data.title: Oracle: Reinventing Itself From System Of Record To AI And Cloud
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the White Falcon Capital Q1 2026 Partner Letter.

Oracle (ORCL) is reinventing itself from a traditional software company into a vertically integrated enterprise cloud provider, controlling the entire stack from infrastructure to database to enterprise applications. At its core, with its database and applications, Oracle is the 'system of record' which executives rely on as the 'single source of truth' within an enterprise. Much of the world's government, financial, healthcare, and retail data runs on Oracle databases, making the platform deeply mission critical and exceptionally sticky. Even in a world where agents autonomously execute business processes, enterprises will always need a 'single source of truth' to reconcile transactions, diagnose failures, and audit agent driven decisions.

Today, Oracle is leveraging this foundation to transform into a specialized cloud and AI business. Their strategy is to use their 'data moat' to pull AI workloads onto their infrastructure business (OCI). Because so much of the world's mission critical information already lives in Oracle databases, the company is using that position to attract AI training and inference workloads onto OCI. It is now the fourth largest hyperscaler but rather than compete head on with AWS, Azure, or Google, Oracle is partnering with them and giving enterprises multicloud flexibility. Interestingly, Oracle's late arrival to the cloud has actually worked in its favor as it could incorporate the industry's best architectural practices for its specific workloads.

Even though this strategic pivot is logical, the market remains skeptical because cloud and AI infrastructure require enormous amounts of upfront capital. Early results are encouraging. Oracle is already generating solid cloud margins at its current scale, growing OCI consumption, and accelerating both its database and enterprise application businesses. Oracle is also being creative and securing billions in upfront payments from customers who essentially pay for their own hardware in exchange for guaranteed access to 'compute'. We believe that the debt/EBITDA ratios for Oracle will be manageable as they build out this infrastructure and consolidated ROIC's will be more than sufficient.

"AI is a five-layer cake... spanning energy, chips and computing infrastructure, cloud data centers, AI models and, ultimately, the application layer."

- Jensen Huang, CEO Nvidia

With OCI, the database, and its applications portfolio, Oracle now participates in three of the five layers of the AI stack. We established our position recently at 20x 2026E EPS, which we view as attractive given the company's quality and the potential upside. This is especially true when considering that its Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) - contracted but not yet delivered revenue - point to accelerating growth in both revenue and earnings going forward.

data.title: Stocks Shook Off The March Dip: Now Q1 Earnings And April Data Take Center Stage
data.description:
Stocks pace for one of their best April performances on record. The 9% S&P 500® pullback from January through the March 30 intraday low may feel like a distant memory for investors. Still, geopolitical risks remain, and macro unease leaves many pundits doubting the rally’s durability. Is a pause in order? Or are we off to the races like we were a year ago?​

Earnings Beat Rates Signal Underlying Corporate Strength​

The answer may lie in key earnings reports over the next few weeks, along with a trove of macro data points that will set the stage for the summer. On the former front, BofA noted that a solid 76% of S&P 500 companies that have already reported topped Wall Street earnings estimates. That's above the typical 68% Week 1 beat rate. Heavy hitters from the Mag 7 kick off the heart of the reporting period—Tesla (TSLA) is on for tonight.​

We’ll be all over the revenue and income data, as well as crucial guidance numbers as they roll in. It's also important to keep an eye on the macro situation at home and abroad. Of course, the conflict in Iran is A-1 material, but incoming March and April economic updates may soon garner increasing attention. Let’s double-click on that situation to help frame both the earnings backdrop and a busy corporate event season already in full swing.​

Data on Tap, And Why They Matter​

Following Tuesday’s March Retail Sales report, Thursday’s set of S&P Global Flash PMIs will be the timeliest vibe check yet. These Purchasing Manager Index readings are for this month and follow a record-setting (in a bad way) April preliminary reading from the University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers. Its sentiment gauge was the worst on record. ​

That’s saying something, as “UMich” dates back to the middle of the last century. The eye-popping report made for the most K-shaped of all macro stats: the S&P 500 hit a record high as consumer sentiment sat at a record low (but at least they are still going to the movies!).​

Consumers Spent Heavily in March (Whether They Wanted to or Not)

UMich Sentiment Plunges to a Record Low

For it to be official, Friday’s UMich survey reading must confirm the preliminary poll from April 10. Given some optimism about a ceasefire deal between the U.S. and Iran (and the potential reopening of the Strait of Hormuz), households’ financial angst could ease.

April Data Offer Post-War Clues​

To be clear, PMIs and consumer sentiment are “soft” data. They are not “hard” data, such as Retail Sales. Additional hard data hits next Tuesday morning, right before major tech companies post Q1 earnings. ​

Two timely data points that point to a still-decent macro backdrop are the ADP Employment Change Weekly and the Johnson Redbook Retail Sales. ADP’s weekly job change gauge tallied its best level over its brief history (since September 2025) earlier this month, while the weekly consumer spending pulse suggests a steady 7% YoY consumption trend. Not bad.​

Marrying the Macro & Micro​

Those are unofficial barometers, but along with updates from management teams at industry conferences and Annual General Meetings, they may help portfolio managers piece together a macro and micro mosaic. Later in the session on Tuesday, April 28, comes The Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence survey. ​

For background, UMich tends to reflect inflation perceptions, while The Conference Board’s questionnaire leans more on the labor market outlook. With a generally steady jobless rate and real wage growth now near the flat line, it’s difficult to be all that rosy about the April report’s possible findings.​

February Personal Income Turned Negative

The Fed Meets Again. Powell’s Last Stand? Warsh Warming Up.​

From there, the Fed meets mid-week and announces its April policy decision next Wednesday afternoon. No rate change is expected. What’s more, this is not a “dot plot” meeting, so fireworks should be few. ​

FOMC drama centers not so much on Chair Powell right now as on his presumptive successor, Kevin Warsh. The former voting member began his confirmation hearing before the Senate Banking Committee yesterday. While confirmation hearings for Federal Reserve chairs are usually staid events, this one has been a bit more lively, shall we say.​

Next Thursday brings another inflation update, this time the PCE Price Index for March. If it feels like we are getting one major macro data point after another, you’re not crazy. Data from last year’s government shutdown is just now getting back on a regular schedule. A soft March PCE print may help tee up a possible Fed rate cut later this year. ​

Recall that the market-implied odds of an ease were nixed as oil soared above $100 per barrel last month. But as energy prices ebb, rate-cut probabilities have ticked back up.​

April CPI Skyrocketed Due to Oil’s Spike; Core Inflation Remains Tame

From there, the first Friday of May brings a first look at Q1 GDP. This will follow a weak third update to the Q4 economic growth report, so it may carry added market influence.​

Q4 GDP: Tepid 0.4% Growth Rate

April Employment & Berkshire Without Buffett In Charge​

After that, it’ll be all about the labor market. The official April jobs report arrives on May 8, which follows “Woodstock for Capitalists” on May 2 (Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, Nebraska).

Low Initial Claims, Falling Continuing Claims Point to Labor Market Improvement

The Bottom Line

It’s a busy macro stretch as company earnings reports come in fast and furious. A focus on real data and earnings may be a welcome development for investors wary of geopolitical headlines. The team at Wall Street Horizon will keep you up to speed with the latest trends, and you can access our industry-leading forward-looking corporate event data to stay ahead of markets.

Original Post

data.title: Rayonier Advanced Materials: Priced For A Success That Isn't There Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Rayonier Advanced Materials Inc. (RYAM) has moved from low $3s to high $11s in the last one year — a sharp 3-4x move driven by expectations of recovery, which is in turn driven by pricing gains in cellulose specialties and expected positive free cash flow in 2026.

But there’s a problem. The valuation is already quite high — too high in my opinion — and has caught up with the recovery narrative. The stock is not trading at distressed multiples any more. It is actually closer to mid-cycle multiples, even though earnings are still depressed. Given their high leverage and still negative FCF, the margin for error is thin.

RYAM currently trades at ~8-9x EBITDA on about $140M earnings. Net debt is above $700M, and FCF is still negative. Management’s strategy is to use the new pricing uptick to expand margins, and maybe become cash flow positive in 2026.

If EBITDA goes up to ~$200M, we could justify a low-to-mid teens price. Anything more than that requires EBITDA approaching $300M. And that needs a near-perfect execution across pricing, costs, and portfolio mix.

That makes further upside from here heavily dependent on how well the company can execute — especially in translating pricing gains into sustained EBITDA increase. The company must also execute cost discipline and deleverage. Such heavy dependence on execution makes the current price less attractive than a year ago. Let's take a look at RYAM ahead of its next earnings release on May 5th.

Rayonier Advanced Materials Business Overview

Rayonier had roughly $1.47B in annual revenue last year, but most of the earnings came from specific segments.

RYAM’s business has five segments, but Cellulose Specialties is the core driver. It typically accounts for ~50-60% of revenue and 70-80% EBITDA (inferred from margin disclosures). Thus, Cellulose is the economic driver for RYAM, producing high-purity cellulose used in applications like cigarette filters, pharmaceuticals, and industrial products.

An important aspect is stickiness. Many of RYAM’s products are customized for specific orders, have long 12-24 months of qualification cycles, and require tight process control. So there’s switching costs and so, contract-based pricing. That helps retain customers. This is also the same segment where the ~18% pricing reset has recently been implemented, so if margins expand, they will do so here.

The rest of the portfolio is different. Cellulose Commodities, Paperboard, and High-Yield Pulp together make up ~40–50% of revenue, but have lower/volatile margins. The reason: margin comes from market-driven pricing, and pricing is tied to global pulp and paper cycles. The company does not have much ability to differentiate on pricing. This is why these lower-margin segments dilute consolidated margins, and also why the overall business cannot command specialty-type multiples.

Rayonier Advanced Materials’ business needs a lot of capital and operational complexity. It runs both kraft and sulfite process types across different feedstocks. That gives RYAM the flexibility to shift production between specialty and commodity. Ultimately, it helps expand margins because they can focus on whatever offers better margins at any point of time.

So RYAM is a hybrid business, with different margins, so its balance sheet amplifies both outcomes. It cannot be valued solely as a specialty business unless they can show that specialties-driven EBITDA growth is both dominant and sustainable.

One news that should be mentioned — because it does give some sentiment support — is that the company recently changed CEOs, and is undergoing a strategic review after an unsolicited interest. Management has indicated it is open to anything, including a sale, or merger, or restructuring. This development comes at a time when the business may be in the early stages of a pricing-led turnaround, although earnings and cash flow are not yet reflecting such a turnaround.

What The Market Is Pricing Today

With $140M EBITDA and 8-9x multiples, RYAM has an enterprise value of $1.1B and trades at around $9. These are not distressed multiples, and they clearly assume current earnings and margins will improve from here. Frankly, the market already thinks EBITDA will move towards the $150-170M range, and is pricing it accordingly.

We can see this from the balance sheet, where the $700M+ net debt makes a large portion of enterprise value belong to creditors, not equity holders. That means, even if EBITDA improves incrementally, that doesn’t fully translate to equity upside.

In other words, the market has already priced for modest improvements. For the stock to move strongly higher from current levels, EBITDA must move so much that some of the debt can be addressed quickly, within 1-2 years. The company also has to go into expansion mode. This means an EBITDA of around $200M range, or even higher.

RYAM Stock Valuation

RYAM stock’s valuation can be framed directly through EBITDA expansion and how that impacts equity.

Under such a framework, the downside case has an EBITDA of ~$120M with a 6x multiple, so enterprise value comes to ~$720M. Subtract net debt of ~$700M from, and we are left with equity value of $0-50M, or a per share price of ~$0-1. In this scenario, the high leverage effectively cripples equity.

If we factor in partial success, we get a base case where EBITDA is ~$200M, and we apply a more positive 8x multiple, so EV becomes $1.6B. Subtracting net debt, we get equity value of ~$900-950M, which gives a per share price of ~$13-15, a 40-60% upside from current levels. This situation can happen if pricing gains translate into margin improvement in Cellulose Specialties, other segments remain anchored — and there is partial rerating.

In an upside, full execution bull case, EBITDA goes up to ~$300M, and we apply a 9x multiple to get enterprise value of $2.7B. Subtracting net debt, we still get an equity value of ~$2.0-2.1B, which translates to a per share price of ~$29–31. This outcome becomes possible with sustained pricing power, stable demand, and very strong cost discipline.

Now, at ~$9 per share (~$400M equity value), the market is effectively pricing in an EV of ~$1.1B, and with an implied EBITDA of $150-170M, it is applying a mid-to-high single digit multiple on forward. That means, moderate improvement is already factored into the price, if not a full turnaround.

This makes the setup highly sensitive to execution. Moving from $140M to $200M EBITDA drives most of the upside, and this can only come from near perfect execution. On the downside, even a modest miss can be amplified by the leverage into very sharp equity compression.

Conclusion

At upcoming earnings, investors need to watch for three signals. First is quarterly EBITDA progression. Can Rayonier Advanced Materials move from ~$140M towards the $180–200M range over the next few quarters? That is the key question. Then we have to watch for free cash flow, and whether there’s a clear shift towards positive cash flow so we know that pricing gains are real and coming through the P&L and balance sheet. Third is net debt reduction, because that leverage is amplifying the earnings stress. Even modest deleveraging can change equity sensitivity. Unless these few things improve, it becomes difficult to justify the current valuation.

At current levels, RYAM is priced for partial success. However, that success is yet to arrive. The three conditions I noted above have to be met, and leverage is also a huge drag on the company. The risk-reward is now skewed towards the downside. For all these reasons, I rate RYAM a Hold at current prices. If the next 1-2 earnings show distinct improvement in all these metrics and clear deleveraging, I may re-rate RYAM.

data.title: Cardinal Energy: Robust Fundamentals, Growth Prospects, Valuation Confirm A Buy
data.description:
Almost a year ago, I made my initial coverage of Cardinal Energy, Ltd. (CRLFF). Its value has already nearly doubled, which justifies my bullish stance. Now, the global economy and energy market have become more volatile than ever amid stubborn inflation and intense geopolitical situations. Yet, CRLFF maintains solid and intact fundamentals while trying to take advantage of potential opportunities in the long run.

Canadian Energy: Safety Despite Volatility

In the latter part of 2025, the energy market has become more volatile amid sharp commodity price swings. This has been evident in the global oil market due to the geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and the offsetting impact of the OPEC oil supply hike that pushed oil prices down to their five-year lows. The impact was also felt by Cardinal Energy Ltd. in its most recent performance. Before I proceed, I'd like to highlight that all numbers except for valuation will be in CAD.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue or petroleum and natural gas revenue amounted to $129.50M, down by 12.4% YoY from $147.78M. This YoY decrease was much lower than in my previous coverage at only 0.2%. Various factors contributed to this notable decrease. One aspect to consider was the continued decrease in WTI Crude Oil price. During the quarter, it dropped to its five-year low of $56.48 per barrel, which was mainly due to the OPEC oil hike. With that, the value of oil CRLFF produced also dropped.

Another consideration was the composition of its production. During Q4 2025, its light/medium crude oil remained the primary component. However, it decreased to 44% of the total versus 48% YoY. Meanwhile, heavy oil rose to 43% versus 38% YoY. Light and medium oil are mostly tied to WTI with some adjustments, so lower WTI prices mean lower light/medium oil revenue. On the other hand, heavy oil is deeply tied to WCS or Western Canadian Select. Note that WCS is priced at a significant discount to WTI due to price differentiation and forex effect. With that, its pricing is typically lower than that of light/medium. It's not surprising that the total revenue decreased that much.

On a lighter note, natural gas had some positive impact due to its strong pricing. In fact, it broke above $5 per MMBtu, which was its two-year high. The only problem was that its impact was limited since it only comprised 9% of the total production. Meanwhile, the operating expenses remained manageable despite the mounting cost pressures amid stubborn inflation. After all, lower oil prices meant lower cost of producing and transporting oil. Of course, these were not enough to offset the decrease in revenues. But these aspects helped CRLFF maintain positive and double-digit profits or margins. In the end, the operating margin remained high at 43.2% despite its decrease from 48.0% YoY.

Middle East Tension: The Good And The Bad

As a business deeply involved in various upstream activities, CRLFF is highly sensitive to oil price swings. And despite the ceasefire deal, volatility is still evident. There are risks associated with a global slowdown or even a recession. After all, higher oil prices mean higher fuel prices, which may exacerbate stubborn inflation. If economic activity slows down as a repercussion, oil demand may also be affected in the process.

On a lighter note, higher oil prices mean higher profits or margins for oil producers. The tension has already sent WTI to its one-year high. Right now, it appears to be under control after returning to $90 per barrel. Even so, it remains much higher on a YoY basis, so there is a huge possibility that it will enjoy higher revenues and margins in H1 2026. More importantly, the tension also led to substitution effect. Some consumers may have switched to other producers to cover their needs. With that, CRLFF may have received or may continue to receive additional oil demand. If the war resumes after the ceasefire agreement, oil prices may rebound, which may push its revenues up again.

Mitigants/Drivers

Amid the offsetting impact of the Middle East tension, I believe it has a lot of risk mitigants and growth drivers. Its high concentration on oil production allows it to benefit from the positive spillovers of the war. I bet the impact will extend to its bottom line earnings.

But a more interesting and overlooked component is its natural gas production. Its prices may have weakened recently, but I believe it offers a lot of growth potential to the company. One aspect to note is the global data center expansion. In 2025-2030, global data center capacity demand is expected to increase by 3.5x. This will require more electricity, which, in turn, will increase the demand for natural gas. If demand exceeds supply, the natural gas price will skyrocket. CRLFF will also benefit from this trend and support revenue growth and margin expansion. Its oil production will receive some spillovers because data centers may need mechanical generators to prevent or mitigate disruptions in case of a power outage. Generators are typically ran by oil or fuel, so CRLFF will enjoy the trend in the long run.

With respect to its finances, its robust liquidity ensures it can sustain its potential expansion to match demand growth in the succeeding years. Its cash and cash equivalents may not be that high at only $4.2B, but it still has $54M receivables to support its liquidity. CRLFF complements it with prudent debt management as it maintains a long maturity profile. In fact, no amount will mature within the year. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.2x ($238.6M/$203M), which means that it only needs 1.2 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To assess its valuation, I decided to use the Dividend Discount Model or DDM. The annualized dividend is $0.52 based on the most recent monthly payout. With that, the dividend growth rate is 10.19% based on the five-year CAGR. I know this model may not be ideal since dividends of CRLFF are volatile. With that, I set its risk premium at 10.0%. I believe this is already very conservative since it is twice the 5.0% market standard. Lastly, I estimated its Beta at 1.02x, which means that the price trend of CRLFF is still in line with the S&P 500, although slightly more volatile. With all this information, I derived a TP or target price of $11.79. This shows that the stock is still reasonably valued with some upside at its current price.

Technicals

CRLFF's technicals are still bullish, although caution is warranted. The price keeps increasing with higher highs and lows, which means that the uptrend still holds. It is also trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which shows that the current momentum is still stronger than in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still increasing above the 200-day SMA, so recent market sentiments have remained optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI warrant some caution because the stock is already overbought. The MACD line is still above the signal line, but the uptrend appears to be flattening, which can tell us that buying volume is decreasing as the stock becomes more expensive. The histogram is still within positive territory but also decreasing, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume but showing more caution. On a lighter note, RSI is still moving in line with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence. Profit-taking and dips are possible after overbuying, but more buyers may enter if opportunities for buying appear again.

Conclusion

Cardinal Energy, Ltd. has a lot of growth prospects to sustain its expansion despite geopolitical tension and energy market volatility. This is supported by its robust fundamentals that guarantee its longevity and dividends. Its valuation is also still reasonable, so buying at this level is still ideal. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals persist despite caution due to overbuying. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple: The CEO Transition Is A Multiple Compression Catalyst
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve been bearish on Apple Inc. (AAPL) for a while, not based on the business itself, which I find simply formidable, but more on the inherent valuation that exceeds the vast majority of its peers, while it does not do enough, in my opinion, to catch up in the AI game right now. Since my last Sell rating, Apple has seen its stock price only increase by 0.69% while the S&P 500 (SP500) rose by 2.65%. With the announcement of the CEO transition as well as the upcoming earnings, I want to dive into the company to see if it is worth changing my overall rating on the tech giant ahead of its earnings release on April 30th.

The new CEO

As Tim Cook steps down from the CEO role, we can only applaud to its operational success as under his tenure, Apple’s market value grew by nearly $3.7T, surpassing the value created by any other American CEO in total dollars, except Nvidia's (NVDA) Jensen Huang. Revenue also quadrupled during his tenure from $100B to $400B. Cook’s most significant achievement has been the development of the services division which includes the App Store, Apple Pay, Apple Music, and iCloud, that provides a high-margin cushion and evolved into a more than $100B annual business. Cook also oversaw the creation of the wearables category that solidified the company’s position as a health and lifestyle brand.

Looking ahead to John Ternus’s tenure, it is a shift from operational and supply chain mastery and more toward engineering and product architecture. He has been a perennial insider as he joined the design team in 2001 and has overseen hardware engineering of every major product line, including the iPhone, iPad, Mac and AirPods. His leadership style has been described as decisive and instant-led, which is relatively different than Cook’s style. His style has been compared to Steve Jobs and this is viewed as an essential by the Board to navigate the current industry transition driven by AI. The transition is also involving a broader shift of the C-suite with the main goal to consolidate power within the engineering organization as Apple doubles down on integrated hardware and silicon capabilities.

Though I believe that Ternus is probably the right man for the job (at the end of the day, the smart people at Apple are sure to make the right decision), his tenure will start at a time when Apple’s stock trades at a high premium and many leadership transitions of the sort have led to multiple compressions. This will especially come if the new CEO cannot immediately resolve concerns about the stalled AI strategy.

Earnings Preview

First, I’ll look at the potential gross margin compression, as Apple guided for an up to 49% gross margin for its fiscal Q2, but the firm flagged rising memory costs and component shortages. If gross margins come in at the lower end or even below the 48% floor, it will signal us that the cost structure is less insulated from the AI CapEx cycle than optimistic investors expect.

I’ll also look at the potential services growth deceleration as during Q1, services grew 14% Y/Y, but if the Q2 numbers come in below that range, mainly if the App Store growth decelerates amid the Supreme Court ruling forcing external payment links, it might undermine the highest-multiple segment in the firm’s portfolio. It will be relevant to see if the firm mentions anything about the Supreme Court decision.

On the supply chain side, Apple’s guidance has assumed the current tariff landscape and no worsening, but with the 145% tariffs on Chinese imports and the reciprocal tariffs hitting India, Vietnam, and other diversification destinations, any forward guidance acknowledging margin pressure would be significant. Morgan Stanley has estimated Apple would need to raise U.S. prices by nearly 18% to fully offset tariff costs.

Of course, I’ll look for the comments on the CEO transition as it will be Cook’s last earnings call as the CEO. If the call is purely about Cook's farewell with no vision articulation by Ternus, then it might reinforce the fact that the transition is undergoing without a clear strategic pivot.

And finally, I’ll look at any commentary on the competition landscape in China, as Apple has been facing the rising threat that is Huawei. To give a backdrop, China is the third-largest market for Apple as iPhone revenue surged 23% in Q1, but it was effectively a launch quarter. Q2 is seasonally weaker and will be the first real test of whether demand was pulled forward or sustainable.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier, my biggest bearish stance is about Apple’s valuation, which currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 34.52, higher than Meta (META) at 22.59 and Microsoft (MSFT) at 27.18, despite these two firms growing their net income by 34.11% and 20.92% CAGR, respectively, over the past 3 years. Apple’s effective re-rating that forced a multiple expansion has been based on the services segment, and the main question is whether this is sustainable.

The growth rate has been decelerating in recent years and the high-margin part of this would be the Google search licensing deal, which is estimated to bring $20 to $25B annually, but it is under direct threat from the DOJ antitrust case against Google. Any change to this arrangement would create a massive revenue gap in the services segment, potentially depressing valuation multiples. Though I’ll say that the firm's FCF yield is relatively satisfactory despite the heightened share price, as it stands at 3.1%.

Risks to my bearish take

My bear case assumes plainly that the high valuation and strategic gaps will lead to a market correction, though Apple possesses a massive and loyal installed base of over 2.5B active devices. This creates an unparalleled recurring revenue engine for services that may remain durable even if hardware growth fluctuates.

And despite component cost inflation, Apple is set to potentially reach a 50% gross margin within 2 years, thanks to the shift toward high-margin Services and the use of in-house silicon to lower hardware costs.

On the leadership side, Ternus is apparently a product architect, unlike the operationally focused Tim Cook. Therefore, his leadership could boost product development and restore a more decisive, Jobs-era focus on breakthrough innovation rather than incremental updates.

Conclusion

John Ternus will inherit a company that is at the height of its power while at the same time its most vulnerable strategic crossroads. The foundation laid by Tim Cook provides the resources necessary to potentially navigate the AI transition. Though the current 34x earnings multiple assumes a level of execution perfection that Apple has failed to demonstrate in its software intelligence division over the past 2 years. This valuation problem persists because of the timing of this transition, and I would not be surprised if the multiple compress through the transition. I rate Apple a Sell.

data.title: My 3 Biggest Mistakes In BDC Investing
data.description:
Over the last four years or so, there has not been a single day when I haven't spent time thinking about, assessing or educating myself about BDCs.

While I was familiar with BDCs way before that, it wasn't until 2022, when the Fed started rapidly raising rates, that BDCs really caught my attention.

The combination (back then) of high yields, income growth and price appreciation, obviously, forced me, as an income-oriented investor, to explore this opportunity set deeper.

The more I dug, the more I realized how misunderstood the asset class is and how it is possible to identify the so-called diamonds in the rough (e.g., durable income and NAV compounders, irrespective of what kind of market environment we have).

So far, I have to say that the net result of my BDC investment journey has been rather successful.

For example, my Top 3 BDC picks - Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC), Capital Southwest (CSWC) and Trinity Capital (TRIN) - have all delivered stable dividends (the key objective of my investment strategy) and clearly outperformed the index (BIZD) this year when "almost everyone" in the media have been plotting "GFC 2.0." scenario for BDCs.

But, of course, the road has not been a straight line higher. There have been several bumps in the road, which have served as great lessons, making me a more seasoned BDC investor.

So, in this article I would like to share my three biggest BDC investing mistakes that have mostly stemmed from my traditional approach as a long-term value (dividend) investor.

These are some really subtle and BDC-specific lessons which can be learned only by having boots on the ground and putting actual capital to work.

Mistake #1: High P/NAV BDCs are overvalued

If we look at BDC core business activity, which is about providing loans to small and medium sized U.S. businesses, then conceptually there should not be huge valuation differences among the sector players.

At the end of the day, their core business activity's upside is bound by collected coupons and repaid principals. The loan investment yields (which generate coupon payments) are quite similar across the board. And in the case of higher yields (and thus risks), there should be higher default rates that would balance things out.

Plus, in this context (credit investments where upside is largely tied to NAV), paying way above NAV sounds rather illogical.

This kind of thinking was the reason why at the very start of my BDC journey, I decided to avoid almost all internally managed BDCs and some select external ones.

Of course, it is the wrong way to think like that.

Just take a look at the chart below, which shows how some of the highest priced (with mostly above NAV multiples) BDCs - CSWC, MAIN and Sixth Street Specialty Lending's (TSLX) - have performed compared to the BDC index:

These are the main reasons why optically richly priced BDCs tend to outperform lower multiple peers:

1) Internally managed BDCs per definition are priced higher than the external peers. This is because they don't charge "shareholder unfriendly" base management and incentive fees, which are similar to the conventional hedge fund model "2% + 20%". Furthermore, since internally managed BDCs have internalized management teams, it means that there is a better shareholder alignment in place, which in the long-run should benefit patient investors.

2) Some BDCs carry high-quality equity co-investments in their portfolios, which introduce equity-like return potential. The upside here is obviously much higher than what could come from simple lending (i.e., it is not bound by NAV). For example, as of Q4 2025, MAIN had ~$7.10 per share in cumulative, pre-tax net unrealized equity appreciation, which accounts for ~16% of the BDC's market cap. It makes sense to pay extra for these kinds of returns.

3) The ability to avoid material credit losses is key. BDCs which have a proven track-record of keeping non-accruals low across different market cycles deserve a premium for that. At least I would pay some premium if I knew that in return I'm getting more sustained and durable NAV (and dividends).

Finally, what we have to appreciate here as well is that P/NAV that is above 1x creates a positive feedback loop for additional value creation. What I mean by that is the BDCs with P/NAV of above 1x can issue (new shares) equity on accretive terms, which can bring in fresh capital for portfolio growth funding. And larger portfolio is what can drive NII per share growth, which, in turn, boosts the resiliency of NAV and base dividends.

Mistake #2: Deep-value BDCs can't fall much lower

Up until relatively recently, my understanding was that deep-value BDCs, which trade at significant discounts to NAV are better protected from an additional downside.

The logic behind this assumption was that the steep discounts should introduce a floor as to how much lower the valuations can fall.

The only condition that would be required is that non-accruals remain relatively stable (e.g., they could even go up as long as they don't approach levels that have been baked into the cake via P/NAV discounts).

However, the reality is different.

CION Investment Corp (CION) and Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) are two great examples - both BDCs which I thought that would not go much lower from the early 2026 levels. At the start of 2026, CION and OTF were trading at P/NAV of 0.84x and 0.70x, respectively.

This is how they have performed since then:

All this has happened despite almost perfectly stable non-accruals in their Q4 2025 earnings reports (both BDCs experienced an uptick of 20 bps in their non-accruals).

The reason why these BDCs are down is related to the perceived risks, which are associated with CION's notable exposure to PIK and OTF's ~70% exposure to SaaS companies. Nothing has materialized so far - nor is it likely to - that would result in major credit losses, at least not anywhere close to what is implied by the current 40% - 50% discounts.

My takeaway from this is clear: when the sentiment turns bearish, deep discounts don't provide extra downside protection. Instead, we could easily make a case for the opposite effect.

Mistake #3: Non-accruals are transitory

This mistake or "lesson learned" goes back to the first point I made in the article above, which was about high P/NAV BDCs actually deserving such multiples.

It just makes sense to pay premium price for a "premium journey" - e.g., smooth dividend and durable NAV ride. These high P/NAV BDCs very rarely report material and systematic non-accruals that would destroy investors' confidence.

This is what we cannot say about heavily discounted names.

FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) and Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) illustrate this nicely.

All three BDCs have been struggling with non-accruals for quite some time. Each quarter that has indicated stabilization in non-accruals have oftentimes followed by a disappointment in the subsequent quarter again (sometime with a couple of quarter lag).

Despite elevated non-accruals going into 2026 (and some positive signals about potential stabilization from management), Q4 2025 earnings reports surprised investors to the downside again:

While I was always skeptical about HRZN and TPVG (see my articles here and here, respectively), in FSK's case I had a different view.

After harvesting 27% in gains from my first bullish piece on FSK back in December 2023 and then avoiding the BDC from January 2025 to October 2025 (FSK dropped by ~33% in this period), I was in a pretty good spot with FSK. However, seeing in October, last year the ~30% discount to NAV and stabilizing non-accruals, I thought that it was a wonderful opportunity to buy the BDC again.

Since then FSK has lost around 22% of its market cap mostly because of yet another negative surprise on the non-accrual front.

The key takeaway here is that non-accruals can very likely be systematic where the consequences of that can manifest themselves in lags (e.g., two steady quarters followed by another negative surprise). Hence, I am no longer considering BDCs which have started to report improving loan quality statistics from levels that are really serious (say, non-accruals above 3%).

Final remarks

BDC investing can be rather counterintuitive.

It might very well turn out that richly priced BDCs continue to outperform, heavily discounted ones fall more than the sector when the sentiment turns bearish and those with attractive P/NAV levels and improving credit losses turn out to be disastrous investments.

In a nutshell, the key thing that I have learned is that it is worth paying higher price for higher quality. Fewer surprises, fewer headaches, and increased dividend and NAV stability, which what matters the most for me as a long-term durable income investor.

data.title: Canadian Solar: Discounted Power Generation And Storage Leader - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
I previously upgraded Canadian Solar (CSIQ) as a cautious Buy in January 2026, thanks to the new data center-related monetization opportunities as they leveraged their solar/battery storage capabilities during the ongoing AI boom.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating here, thanks to the great insights from their growing backlog/manufacturing capacity and the discounted valuations.

CSIQ Faces Numerous Headwinds & Tailwinds Entering FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, the bears have proven my Buy rating wrong, with CSIQ breaching the prior trading floor of $20s to return nearly all of their October/November 2025 gains to the $13s by the time of writing.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

With CSIQ set to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on May 14, 2026, I urge readers to monitor their performance against the previously offered guidance across:

  • revenues of $1B at the midpoint (-16.6% QoQ/-16.6% YoY),
  • gross margins of 14% (-3.8 points QoQ/+2.3 YoY),
  • solar module shipment of 2.3 GW (-46.5% QoQ/-66.6% YoY), and
  • battery shipment of 1.8 GWh (-10% QoQ/+125% YoY).

Given the mixed prospects on a QoQ/YoY basis, it is unsurprising that CSIQ has already lost much of their October/November 2025 gains by the time of writing.

These reasons are also why I have warned about its potentially lumpy performance metrics at a time of changing US regulatory landscape, as observed in the "prolonged solar downturn."

2. Solar

Even then, I am of the opinion that CSIQ is likely to remain a long-term winner in the solar sector, attributed to the robust insight offered by their Solar Development Project Pipeline of 24.41 GWp in FY2025 (-2.1% YoY).

This is on top of the management's guidance for the US solar shipment at 6.75 GW in FY2026 (+11.2% YoY, based on 25% of its FY2025 global shipments at 24.3 GW) and the US battery shipment at 5 GWh.

These numbers underscore the robust demand for their domestically made solar modules, which perhaps explain why CSIQ has decided to:

  • double their US "nameplate capacity to 10 gigawatt peak by the end of 2026," up from the current annual production run rate of over 5 GW, and
  • expand their US solar cell capacity from the original plan of 5 GW to 6.3 GW peak,

with the expanded capacity expected to the make the company "the largest crystalline silicon solar module manufacturer in the country" upon completion by the end of 2026.

These may allow CSIQ to better serve the "strategic high-value markets" while driving improved top/bottom-line performance, with market analysts already expecting the "cumulative US solar capacity to nearly triple from 279 GWdc installed at year-end 2025 to 769 GWdc installed by 2036, with average annual capacity additions of more than 44 GWdc."

3. e-Storage

At the same time, given:

  • the numerous energy storage contracts and deliveries announced on a YTD basis (here, here, here, and here, amongst others) along with
  • the growing contracted backlog of $3.6B in FY2025 (+12.5% YoY) and the operating projects contracted under LTSA at 29 GWh (+491.8% YoY),

I am of the opinion that CSIQ may report robust e-Storage performance metrics in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

These developments lend credibility to the management's plans to embark on capex expansion in "Southeastern Asia for the energy storage production and the lithium battery energy storage production" in 2026, with these capacity "mainly prepared for U.S."

Readers may also want to note that the US Supreme Court has struck down the prior tariff rates, with the newly imposed tariff of 10% being notably more reasonable, with the improved economics from the lower import tariffs perhaps contributing to the "strategically delaying progress at our battery cell and BESS production facility in Shelbyville, Kentucky."

As a result of these developments, I am of the opinion that the stock's steep meltdown by -34.6% post FQ4'25 earnings call (at its worst) has been overly done.

This is given the promising solar/battery demand within the US, the consequently growing production capacity, and the management's reiterated gross margins of over 20% for their solar/battery business segments.

4. Integrated Total Power Solution

While nascent, CSIQ has also hinted at their ambitions to further expand their existing capability beyond the current:

  • CSI Solar, "focused on solar modules and battery energy storage manufacturing and products,"
  • Recurrent Energy, "focused on utility-scale solar power and battery energy storage project development and operation," and
  • the "complex grid interconnection and commissioning and long-term service expertise,"

across

  • the behind-the-meter generation on the client side, which is "producing power directly at or near the data center site" in order to "deliver continuous, dispatchable power, supplying the full load or supplementing the grid during peak demand" and
  • the front-of-the-meter generation on the utility side, which connects power generation/energy storage to the utility infrastructure while stabilizing "the load requirement from the data center storage parts from the grid,"

with the strategic total power solution approach likely to expand their Independent Power Producer [IPP] total addressable market.

Thanks to the clarity from the robust backlog and their likely to be resilient profit margins, the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising, with CSIQ expected to report a more than decent top-line growth at a CAGR of +13.4% through FY2028, while potentially delivering positive adj EPS over the next few years.

These developments underscore their ability to mitigate the challenging regulatory environment, thanks to the ongoing AI boom and the consequently, robust demand for diversified renewable/clean energy generation at a time of aging power grid in the US, including solar.

CSIQ Remains A Great, Contrarian Buy Here

For now, CSIQ has notably lost all of its October/November 2025 gains albeit with the bulls seemingly defending an uptrend support line since the April 2025 bottom.

If anything, CSIQ has also entered oversold territories by the time of writing, across the RSIs and the trading volumes, with it implying a potential bottoming at the current level of $13s, as similarly observed in the sideways trading since late March 2026.

As a result of the -61.9% correction from the 52 week highs, CSIQ looks rather compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.09x, down from the recent high of 1.29x and still cheap against the sector median of 3.18x.

When comparing CSIQ's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.09x and the consensus revenue growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +13.4% to its IPP peers with diversified power generation portfolios across solar, wind, nuclear, natural gas, storage, and others, including:

  • Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) at 12.04x/+22.2%,
  • NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE) at 9.44x/+10.8%, and
  • Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN) at 10.61x/+12.1%, respectively,

it is apparent that CSIQ is even more discounted after the selloff, likely attributed to its debt laden operations at a growing net debt of $4.51B (+61.6% YoY) and a net debt of adj EBITDA ratio of 7.54x (against a year ago at 4.15x).

Given their lumpy cash flows and the likely to be elevated capex activities in FY2026, I am of the opinion that its balance sheet health is likely to further deteriorate in the near-term, before incrementally improving from FY2027, once their expanded capacity is accretive to the top/bottom-line performance.

As a result of the great insights from their growing backlog/manufacturing capacity, the discounted valuations, and the technical bottoming, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CSIQ stock here.

It goes without saying that the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory and risk appetite, attributed to their likely to be lumpy bottom-line performance and deteriorating balance sheet in the near-term, worsened by the still elevated short interest ratio of 21.1% at the time of writing.

data.title: TimesSquare U.S. Small Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Representative Commentary — 1Q26

Performance
Annualized Annualized Annualized Annualized Annualized
1Q26 1YR 3YR 5YR 7YR 10YR
TimesSquare U.S. Small Cap Growth Composite (Gross) -6.47% 10.37% 8.06% 1.56% 7.01% 10.10%
TimesSquare U.S. Small Cap Growth Composite (Net) -6.71% 9.29% 6.99% 0.55% 5.95% 9.02%
Russell 2000® Growth Index -2.81% 23.58% 12.25% 1.62% 7.67% 9.78%

Please see the important performance and other related disclosures at the end of this Commentary, which are an integral part of this quarterly Commentary Newsletter.

In the first quarter, markets navigated a complex backdrop of geopolitical tensions and economic resilience, alongside temporary global tariffs following a Supreme Court decision. Prior to the Iran conflict, markets had reached all-time highs with lower volatility, a dynamic that reversed following U.S. and Israeli engagement. Oil prices spiked, supply chains were disrupted, financial conditions tightened, and investors shifted toward defensive business models, AI capital expenditure beneficiaries, as well as chemical and energy businesses positioned to benefit from higher commodity prices. The conflict in Iran further underscored the growing importance of national resilience, prompting a re-evaluation of global supply chains and energy dependencies. Central banks largely held policy steady in response to energy-driven inflation, with both the Federal Reserve and the ECB adopting a wait-and-see approach. Meanwhile, Japan’s snap election delivered political stability and a mandate for fiscal expansion, despite ongoing concerns around long-term debt sustainability.

Amidst first-quarter volatility, the portfolio underperformed the Russell 2000® Growth Index. A market rotation towards Energy, Materials, Regional Banks, and Utilities and out of Software created an unfavorable backdrop for portfolio performance.

Our preferences in the consumer-oriented sectors lean toward value-oriented or specialty retailers, franchise models, premium brands, or support services for other consumer companies. Valvoline Inc. (VVV) offers engine and automotive maintenance products and services. The combination of a better-than-expected quarter and accelerating same-store sales resulted in a 16% lift in the stock price. BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings Inc. (BJ) operates membership warehouse clubs in the eastern half of the U.S. Its shares rose 9% due to a strong fourth quarter, which included better same-store sales and higher gross margins. Wingstop Inc. (WING), a franchiser and operator of chicken wings-focused restaurants, retreated -35%. While fourth-quarter comparisons were better than feared, underlying trends softened. This can be attributed to economic challenges faced by lower-income consumers, including high gasoline prices. We decided to trim back the position.

Often, the Energy sector moves in lockstep with the underlying commodity prices. Here, we aim to find companies that are either low-cost exploration and production companies with high-yielding fields, or specialized service providers. Matador Resources Co. (MTDR), an exploration and production company with operations in Southeast New Mexico and West Texas, surged ahead 50%. They reported a slight miss on fourth-quarter profit estimates, but production beat. Importantly, management remains committed to capital efficiency as evidenced by longer laterals and improved drilling times. Excelerate Energy (EE) offers liquefied natural solutions including floating storage and regassification units. Fourth-quarter results were in line with Street projections, which boosted the stock by 19%. Management is being conservative with 2026 guidance. The CEO is bullish on their growth prospects as an LNG regasification infrastructure player.

In the Financials sector, we tend to avoid banks experiencing credit deterioration or rising deposit costs, preferring asset managers, specialized insurance companies, or financial technology providers. Victory Capital (VCTR) operates as an asset management company offering specialized investment strategies. Their stock price gained 5% after earnings for the fourth quarter beat the consensus and the company formally abandoned its bid to acquire Janus Henderson Group with a share repurchase authorization. The company repurchased $50 million of shares in the quarter. Hamilton Lane (HLNE) engaged in the provision of private markets investment solutions. A negative FT article in February, largely based on selective interpretation and the omission of facts, triggered a -26% sell-off. The company's fourth-quarter results beat consensus estimates, aided by higher management fees. The alternative investment space has generally been tracking lower on concerns surrounding private credit redemptions and optimistic marks on software investments; however, our granular analysis of the company's exposures suggests that the decline in the stock is overdone.

Our preferences among Health Care stocks are those companies providing novel therapies for unmet needs that command premium pricing, or specialized service providers. Guardian Pharmacy Services Inc. (GRDN), a pharmacy services company provider to long-term health care facilities, improved 25%. Their latest quarter came in ahead of consensus, with management noting higher patient acuity. CG Oncology Inc. (CGON) is a late-stage clinical biopharmaceutical company focused on developing therapeutics to treat bladder cancer. Their lead asset, Cretostimogene (Creto) is well-positioned as a potential best-in-class broad adjuvant therapy to treat non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). The company recently announced that a pivotal trial studying Creto in the intermediate risk segment of the market will read out one year ahead of schedule, potentially allowing access to an additional 35,000 patient segment of the NMIBC market. Anticipation of positive data served to lift the stock by 64%. Mirum Pharmaceuticals Inc. (MIRM) is a biopharmaceutical company focused on developing novel therapies for liver disease. Its shares rose 17% due to strong sales of Livmarli, an oral medication used to treat cholestatic pruritus. It works to lower bile acid levels in the body. Stevanato Group (STVN) supplies drug containment, drug delivery, and diagnostic solutions to the biopharmaceutical industry. Its shares declined by -32% on market concerns that the shift to oral formulations could slow growth in injectable products. However, we believe these concerns may be overblown as Stevanato has several multi-year contracts representing strong revenue visibility. Repligen Corp. (RGEN) is a global life sciences company offering bioprocessing technologies and solutions used in manufacturing biological drugs. Fourth quarter revenues and earnings were ahead of the consensus. Forward guidance factored in uncertainty regarding biopharmaceutical industry capital expenditures and the pace of FDA approvals causing the stock price to retreat by -28%.

Many of our Industrials positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, equipment enabling automation and efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services. Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX), a provider of power and control motion products, climbed 34% on the back of impressive orders for its newly developed data center power management solutions. Mixed results included a slight beat on earnings and a slight miss on revenue. Orders were, however, a standout, leaping 54% year-over-year, aided by wins in the company's new datacenter power management offering. RBC Bearings (RBC) manufactures engineered precision bearings, components, and systems. The stock price rose 21% after they reported fiscal third-quarter earnings and revenues that outpaced the consensus. Legence Corp. (LGN), which is engaged in mission-critical systems in buildings, jumped 31%. Its fourth-quarter results were well above consensus estimates, and it increased forward guidance. Casella Waste Systems Inc. (CWST) operates as a vertically integrated solid waste services company. While fourth quarter results and guidance were generally in line, weaker optics around third-party landfill volume and price caused the stock to tumble -19%. We view the 2026 outlook as conservative. New to the sector this quarter was SOLV Energy Inc. (MWH), a provider of infrastructure services to the power industry.

Among the wide variety of Information Technology companies, we prefer critical system providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve productivity or efficiency for their clients, and others that are growing their shares of corporate IT budgets. MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI) provides analog semiconductor solutions for use in wireless and wireline applications across the radio frequency, microwave, millimeter wave, and Lightwave spectrum. Solid first fiscal quarter results drove the stock up 30%. All their businesses exhibited growth, led by the datacenter segment. Onto Innovation Inc. (ONTO), a semiconductor inspection and metrology company, advanced 30%. Revenues in the latest quarter were in line with Street expectations. Management highlighted strong visibility through 2026 and 2027, with backlog doubling over the past three months. Lattice Semiconductor Corp. (LSCC) develops semiconductor, silicon-based, silicon-enabled products including programmable gate arrays. A solid quarter was rewarded with a 26% rise in the stock price. Management highlighted that the demand signals strengthened over the last quarter, and first quarter guidance was well ahead of projections. Robust demand for data centers and AI-related physical infrastructure supported revenue upside. OneStream Inc. (OS) delivers a unified, AI-enabled, and extensible software platform. The company entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by private equity firm Hg in an all-cash transaction. Its shares gained 29% while held in the quarter. JFrog Ltd. (FROG) offers a platform for software supply management. It continues to experience significant cloud growth, driven by increased AI adoption and higher data-transfer intensity. Despite reporting a strong fourth-quarter 2025 beat, the stock fell -25% after being caught in the "Claude trade" sell-off. As has been common recently, investors reacted to AI-related fears first and assessed fundamental reality later. ServiceTitan Inc. (TTAN) provides an end-to-end, cloud-based software platform that streamlines and automates a wide range of business workflows for skilled tradespeople (i.e., plumbers, electricians, HVAC technicians), including advertising, call answering, job scheduling, and other administrative functions. Its stock declined -41% in concert with the broader software space amid AI disintermediation fears. The company has been gaining traction in new markets, supported by recent enterprise wins and increased adoption of its Pro products and AI virtual agents which are answering phones and booking appointments during off hours. We added to the position on weakness. We exited the Amplitude Inc. (AMPL) position, which offers an AI analytics platform that helps analyze customer behavior within digital products. The market has grown skeptical of seat-based models in this environment. The stock was down -46% while held in the quarter. New to the sector this quarter was Rambus Inc. (RMBS), a manufacturer of high-performance memory subsystems used in Dynamic Random-Access Memory modules. These components help improve signal integrity and are used primarily in CPU server applications that should benefit from the rise in agentic AI adoption.

Within the real estate sector, National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA) is a real estate investment trust focused on the ownership, operation, and acquisition of self-storage properties. Public Storage announced the acquisition of National Storage in an all-stock transaction. We trimmed the position on the strength of its 36% stock price appreciation. New to the sector this quarter was Cushman & Wakefield Ltd. (CWK), a provider of commercial real estate services including facilities management, project development, transaction management, and workplace consulting services.

We are moving into a world where capital is increasingly directed by strategic necessity rather than pure economic returns, transforming energy, defense, and supply chains into critical instruments of national policy. Our investment team are closely monitoring interest rate paths, geopolitical developments in Iran, and the evolving regulatory landscape following recent trade-related legal rulings. While these macro forces and shifting trade frameworks create headlines and near-term volatility, they also generate the dislocations where our fundamental, bottom-up approach thrives. We remain focused on identifying durable business models with disciplined management teams capable of compounding value by navigating this new era of complexity. As always, we are available to answer any questions you may have.

Benchmark returns are not covered by the report of independent verifiers.

Performance Calculations

The performance figures shown are calculated in U.S. dollars on a size-weighted basis and reflect the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings, and the deduction of brokerage commissions and other transaction costs. Performance is provided on a gross basis (before the deduction of management fees) as well as net of the highest fee level from the standard fee schedule listed for this strategy during the period presented. Investment advisory fees generally charged by TimesSquare are described in Part 2A of its Form ADV. This composite may contain some accounts that have used performance based fees. To illustrate performance net of fees, assume $20,000,000 is placed under management for ten years sustaining 10% compound gross total return. If an advisory fee of 1.00% of average assets under management is charged per year, for each year of the ten-year period, the resulting compound annual return would be reduced to 9.0%. The ending dollar value of the account would be $47,347,274 compared with the unreduced account value of $51,874,849.

Internal dispersion is calculated using the equal-weighted standard deviation of all accounts included in the composite on a gross basis for the entire year; it is not presented for periods less than one year or when there were five or fewer portfolios in the composite for the entire year.

The three-year annualized standard deviation measures the variability of the composite and the benchmark returns on a gross basis over the preceding 36-month period. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request. data.title: Pfizer Could Surprise Again This Earnings Season (Preview)
data.description:
Previewing Pfizer's Upcoming Q1 Earnings Release

My previous update on Pfizer (PFE) was essentially a preview article for the firm's then-upcoming Q4 release in February 2025. I kept my "Buy" rating in place, and although the stock opened with a huge gap down when the Q4 data came out (almost 5% below its previous close), Pfizer quickly recovered and is now trading 2-3% above its pre-Q4 price.

The company releases its Q1 earnings on May 5th, in less than 2 weeks, so I think it's time for me to update my reasoning and take a look at what's coming next for PFE.

First off, let me briefly refresh your memories regarding the Q4 results. For the full year 2025, Pfizer had ~$62.6 billion in consolidated revenues (-2% YoY), which looks like a clear stagnation at first glance, but if we exclude the impact of Comirnaty and Paxlovid (COVID drugs), the firm's top line would have been up by 6%, which is actually pretty solid given the entity's size. The non-COVID drug portfolio is gaining traction right now.

In Q4 in particular, we've seen $17.56 billion in revenues (-1.1% YoY) and an adjusted EPS of $0.66 (+4.76% YoY). Both numbers beat the consensus estimates by 4.33% and 16.18%, respectively, according to Seeking Alpha. What I find interesting here is that the revenue decline started to improve when we compare the Q4 results with the full-year dynamics, and at the very same time, the adjusted EPS even went up (albeit by less than 5%), which is already a great start to the turnaround. The market and Wall Street analysts in particular still refuse to see that turnaround in PFE's profitability - the firm has been beating the consensus estimates multiple consecutive quarters, with an average beating size of over 20-30%:

Lower-margin pandemic-era drugs are clearly fading away from the revenue mix, and that's why Pfizer's gross margins have risen significantly (from 49.25% in 2023 to 76% as of 2025). The OPEX is also getting better thanks to the cost control/cutting programs that I discussed in my previous articles on PFE. What we need to see next is management's execution on the commercialization of the existing product pipeline - the top line should start moving in the right direction after a prolonged period of stagnation to allow the market to finally reprice PFE to the upside.

Pfizer has been trying to reshape the duopoly-like obesity market, where Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) have their well-known selling products and heavily monetize them. To rival them, Pfizer bought Metsera for $7 billion, now owning a highly differentiated pipeline of clinical-stage injectable and oral obesity candidates - the story should eventually turn bullish for Pfizer this year, in my opinion, as Pfizer initiates 10 Phase 3 trials for PF'3944 (formerly MET-097i), an ultra-long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist that promises true once-monthly dosing.

The obesity market is big enough for all three companies to enjoy elevated growth rates in the next few years, but beyond obesity, Pfizer has a highly productive 2026-2027 period for its pipeline, having 20 key pivotal Phase 3 studies.

Pfizer plans to initiate/advance 20 pivotal studies in 2026, which include 10 pivotal studies for the ultra-long-acting obesity candidates added from the Metsera acquisition and four for PF-08634404, a dual PD-1/VEGF inhibitor in-licensed from Chinese biotech 3SBio in 2025. Pfizer expects 2026 to be a catalyst-rich year, with some key data-readouts expected in the obesity and oncology programs.

Source

Among the most anticipated Phase 3 data readouts, Pfizer has sigvotatug vedotin (an antibody-drug conjugate for non-small cell lung cancer), Elrexfio (for double-class exposed relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma), and data for its Lyme disease vaccine candidate. If we quickly research the monetary potential for these end markets, we'll quickly realize that all of them are projected to reach tens of billions in TAM by 2030-35, which is giving Pfizer a pretty solid replacement base for the upcoming LOEs (if trials go smoothly and the firm starts to monetize shortly).

Another near-term bullish catalyst I see is a potential major regulatory win this year for Pfizer's Padcev (they got it following the Seagen acquisition). According to PFE's press release, the FDA has already granted Priority Review to Padcev (in combination with Keytruda) as a perioperative treatment for MIBC (muscle-invasive bladder cancer) regardless of cisplatin eligibility, with a target action date of August 17, 2026. So, if it gets approved, Pfizer will get a massive bullish revenue catalyst for 2027, having Padcev as the first and only perioperative treatment for this patient population.

We don't have to cancel out the ongoing cost-cutting, which is not going to stop - for 2026-2027, the management guided for a ramped-up enterprise-wide cost-saving initiatives with $7.7 billion in total savings through 2027, which is a lot for a firm that has annual OPEX at $56-57 billion. They first need to finish the "Realigning Our Cost Base" program by the end of 2026, and then work on the "Manufacturing Optimization Program" (additional $1.5 billion in net savings). Plus, they can save more from AI enablement for their R&D pipeline, and it's not currently priced in by the analysts, in my opinion.

Now, let's switch to the closest catalyst - the upcoming earnings release. For the full year 2026, the management team sees $59.5-62.5 billion with adjusted EPS of $2.80-3.00, incorporating $5 billion in expected COVID-19 product revenues plus ~$1.5 billion in revenue compression due to generic entries. The management guided for the full year, and I couldn't find the Q1 targets. The past beats show a near-perfect track record for the EPS (8 EPS beats and 0 misses over the past 2 years), while on the top line, we see 7 beats and only 1 miss over the same timeframe, which is also quite solid. Despite that, analysts are increasingly leaning bearish leading into the next few quarters, as we see massive 5-15% downward quarterly revisions over the past 6 months:

Pfizer's past beats were mainly supported by aggressive cost control. In the previous call, we've seen the management raising its overall cost savings target to a massive $7.7 billion through 2027, so I don't think Pfizer's margins will drop as harshly as it's currently priced in. In my opinion, Pfizer now has a much thicker margin cushion, so to speak, to drive its earnings beats even if top-line revenue simply meets expectations. That's why I expect the Q4-like beating for the upcoming quarter as well, and also some positive guidance updates around the non-COVID portfolio. They previously guided at ~4% YoY growth there (excluding LOE impacts), so if we hear that 4% again (or better 5-6%), that will become a clear trigger for repricing.

A few words about the valuation side. I know P/E is probably not everyone's best metric when it comes to valuing pharma giants like Pfizer, but I'll take it for simplicity and conventionalism. Right now, PFE is trading at 9.212x forward P/E, which is 2-3 times below the TTM averages of the past 5-10 years.

According to Seeking Alpha, compared with most stocks in the healthcare sector, PFE is priced at a 46-48% discount on forward P/E, depending on whether we use the non-GAAP or GAAP figures.

The consensus says PFE is going to experience a massive earnings contraction over the next few years, which is why the above discount is there:

We've seen constant beats in the past quarters, and if PFE manages to show just 1-2% YoY growth in EPS for 2026 with all the cost-cutting and new programs, a P/E range of 10-12x should be fair, considering the current pipeline and existing scale (even if LOE eats away at some critical drug sales in the next few years). So, assuming only 1.5% YoY rise for FY2026 EPS, and targeting 11x, I see a fair price per share of $35.95:

$3.22 * 1.015 * 11x = ~$35.95/share

Source: Oakoff's calculations

Today, one can buy PFE for $27.31 apiece, so based on my updated model, there's an upside potential of ~32%.

Risks

The patent cliff, as people call it, is the biggest risk that I see to any PFE bullish thesis. There are plenty of LOEs (loss of exclusivity) between 2026 and 2028, when the company is going to lose $15 billion to $18 billion in annual revenue, on average. Blockbusters like Eliquis (anticoagulant, $7.9B in 2025 sales), Ibrance (breast cancer, $4.1B), and Xtandi (prostate cancer, $2.1B) will all face generic competition around 2027, so it's not going to be a one-off risk-off for Pfizer, which is why the consensus paints EPS YoY losses up to the end of fiscal 2029. What I think the consensus is missing is the monetization potential of the current pipeline over the same time, but it's a different story - we're talking about potential here, that might not materialize, while LOEs are inevitable.

So, if the consensus is right and I'm wrong, then Pfizer might adjust to the downside from here as its valuation multiples keep compressing further. At 8x earnings and no adjustments made to the FY2026 EPS consensus, the fair value falls to $23.68/share, which is 13-14% below today's stock price.

Your Takeaway

Despite the clear LOE risks, to which we can add competition risks on their new end markets (mainly obesity), I think Pfizer looks solid ahead of its yet another earnings print. There's a strong track record of beating the consensus estimates, and seeing that there were no bullish revisions following Pfizer's bullish updates in Q4, I assume the history is very likely to repeat itself again. I have a price target of over $27/share, which leads PFE to an upside potential of about 30%. That's why I'm reiterating my previously assigned "Buy" rating today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Can The Market Look Past Coming Headwinds?
data.description:
Yesterday, stocks finally gave back some gains over concerns that the deadline for the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran would come and go today with no resolution. President Trump warned earlier in the day that there would be no extension and that bombing would resume with no deal. Vice President Vance was prepared to board a flight to Pakistan to engage in a second round of talks. At the last minute, his trip was cancelled because Iran had no intentions of showing up. Nor did the regime seem concerned about the end of the ceasefire. Unsurprisingly, after the market close, Trump extended the ceasefire indefinitely to allow mediators in Pakistan to encourage Iran to present a proposal to the Trump administration. Still, Iran has not responded on that front either, and overnight, the IRGC attacked two vessels for violating its blockade.

Iran refuses to engage until the President lifts the blockade on the Strait of Hormuz for its vessels, which he has refused to do. Therefore, oil prices remain elevated, with WTI climbing above $90, while Brent inches back towards $100. It is not just the supply of crude oil that is constrained, but also fertilizer, helium, and liquefied natural gas. Iran is playing the waiting game, as it knows the longer the strait remains closed and prices remain elevated, the more economic pressure it puts on the global economy and the Trump administration. The ball seems to be in President Trump’s court, but the clock is ticking.

Stocks rose after hours on news about the extension of the ceasefire, but that seems shortsighted to me, ignoring the coming headwinds that strengthen each day the waterway remains closed. Granted, retail sales soared in March by 1.7%, largely due to gasoline sales, but core sales were also impressive, increasing 0.6%. This strength in the face of rising gas prices had a lot to do with extra income from tax refunds, but that one-time payment has a temporary benefit.

The strong retail sales figure lifted the Atlanta Fed’s first-quarter economic growth estimate to 1.2%, but the primary engine of our economy, the consumer, has lost strength over the past three quarters, as consumer spending (PCE) contributed 2.3% to overall growth in the third quarter of last year, followed by 1.3% in the fourth. The contribution in the first quarter is just 0.95%.

Both gauges of inflation (CPI and PCE price index) are expected to rise in the coming months as higher energy prices and transportation expenses lift the rates. The longer energy prices remain elevated, the more likely they are to feed through to other goods and services. The CPI was at 3.3% in March, while average hourly earnings grew 3.5%. Accounting for the number of hours worked, inflation-adjusted (real) weekly take-home pay grew just 0.2% last month.

It seems inevitable to me that workers, who are also consumers, will see real wages decline during the second quarter. Tax refunds will soften the blow in April to a degree, but a decline in real wage growth has historically led to a deceleration in real consumer spending growth. This is bound to result in a very slow rate of economic growth in the second quarter. This is why every day that the Strait remains closed is another small blow to our economy. Aside from the technology sector, it should also weigh on earnings expectations, but analysts have yet to adjust their numbers.

Can the stock market look past these headwinds? I think we have seen the correction lows for the year, but I am very suspicious of any strength above recent highs until markets account for the temporary period of slower growth and higher inflation ahead. This certainly isn’t going to lead to an uptick in the labor market, as businesses that were contending with tariffs last year must now manage a sharp increase in input costs from energy, transportation, and other materials impacted by the disruption to global supply chains. Not only are food prices rising, but industrial metal prices are inching higher, with aluminum and copper up 10% since mid-March. This is probably why the latest NFIB small business survey showed capital investment plans at their lowest level since 2009. If this leads to a rise in unemployment, along with slower growth and an increasing inflation rate, we have a recipe for a period of stagflation. The Strait of Hormuz needs to reopen soon.
